name: Lodge L10SK3 Pre-Seasoned Cast Iron Skillet, 12-Inch

specs.Material: Cast iron, pre-seasoned with soy-based vegetable oil
specs.Oven Safe: Up to 500°C (no temperature limit with proper care)

whatWorks: Cast iron's heat retention is unmatched for searing — once the skillet reaches temperature, adding a cold steak doesn't drop the surface temperature the way stainless or nonstick does. The result is a deep, even sear crust that other materials don't reliably produce at home.

The pre-seasoning works well out of the box and improves continuously with use — the more you cook with oil, the more non-stick the surface becomes over months and years. Many owners report their 5-year pans are dramatically more non-stick than when new.

Induction, gas, electric coil, glass-top, oven, broiler, campfire, and grill — this skillet works on all of them without any accessory or modification. Moving from stovetop sear to oven finish is seamless.

With basic care (dry thoroughly, rub with a thin layer of oil), cast iron is essentially indestructible. Lodge pans are regularly passed down across generations, and the seasoning only improves with age.
worthKnowing: At 7.6 lb (3.4 kg), this is a heavy daily cooking pan. Owners with wrist or grip strength limitations frequently find it tiring to use one-handed over a stove — flipping or tilting the pan to baste requires real arm strength.

After washing, the pan must be dried immediately and thoroughly (ideally on low heat on the stove for a minute) and rubbed with a very light layer of oil before storage. Skipping the drying step even once can lead to surface rust, which is fixable but annoying.

Cast iron heats slowly and unevenly on electric coils compared to gas — hot and cool spots are noticeable when cooking eggs or delicate items. Gas or induction minimizes this; electric coil users may find it frustrating for precision cooking.

Acidic foods (tomatoes, citrus, wine sauces) cooked for more than a few minutes can strip seasoning and impart a metallic taste into the food. Long-simmered tomato sauces should stay in stainless or enamelled cookware.

commonComplaints.title: So heavy it's hard to use one-handed…
commonComplaints.body: So heavy it's hard to use one-handed. I love it for steaks and cornbread but I avoid it when I'm tired or rushed because lifting and maneuvering 8 pounds is actually tiring.

commonComplaints.title: Got surface rust the first week…
commonComplaints.body: Got surface rust the first week because I left it slightly damp after washing. Had to re-season it. Now I dry it on the stove every time but I wish the instructions were clearer about this.

commonComplaints.title: Uneven heating on my glass-top stove…
commonComplaints.body: Uneven heating on my glass-top stove — the centre gets much hotter than the edges. Works fine for searing where I want centre heat, but scrambled eggs are a mess.

commonComplaints.title: Ruined a tomato sauce cooking it too…
commonComplaints.body: Ruined a tomato sauce cooking it too long in this pan — everything tasted metallic. Learned the hard way that acidic foods strip the seasoning.

verdict: The Lodge 12-inch cast iron skillet is a $49 pan that will outlast every other piece of cookware in a Canadian kitchen and improve with every use — its heat retention, oven versatility, and longevity are genuinely difficult to match at any price. The weight and maintenance requirements are real trade-offs, and its limitations with acidic foods and electric coil heating are worth knowing. For searing, cornbread, shallow frying, and oven-finishing, nothing in this price range comes close. Buyers willing to learn the basic care routine will likely pass it to their children.

realBuyers.quote: Best sear I've ever gotten on a steak at home. The crust was restaurant-quality. I've tried stainless and nonstick and neither comes close.
My grandmother had this pan for forty years. She gave it to my mother, who gave it to me. The seasoning just keeps getting better.
I camp with it, use it on the grill, finish things in the oven. One pan, everything. Nothing else I own does that.
Made skillet cornbread last weekend. Came out with a perfect crust bottom that no other pan can replicate. It's a different category of cooking.

metaDescription: Lodge 12-inch Cast Iron Skillet review for Canada: pre-seasoned, induction-compatible, made in USA, lasts generations. Honest on weight, rust risk, and acidic foods. ~$49 CAD.
introText: The Lodge L10SK3 12-inch cast iron skillet is the best-value piece of lifetime cookware available in Canada — pre-seasoned from the factory, induction and oven compatible, and made in the USA at a price that makes it difficult to justify any alternative. Available on Amazon.ca (ASIN B00G2XGC88) at around $49 CAD.
